title: Java Methods
description: Master reusable code blocks in Java -- defining methods, parameters, return types, overloading, scope, and recursion -- with 60 hands-on exercises designed for neurodivergent learners.
learning_outcomes: Define methods with correct syntax: access modifier, return type, name, and parameters
Write void methods that perform actions without returning values
Write methods that return values of any type (int, double, String, boolean, arrays)
Pass arguments to methods by value and understand how Java handles parameter passing
Use method overloading to create multiple versions of the same method with different parameters
Understand variable scope -- local variables, parameters, and their lifetime
Apply the DRY principle (Don't Repeat Yourself) by extracting repeated code into methods
Write recursive methods that call themselves to solve problems
Design methods with single responsibility -- each method does ONE thing well
Build complete programs by composing multiple small, well-named methods

ai_tutor_config.personality: Encouraging, patient, uses real-world analogies like recipes, vending machines, and factory workers. Never judges. Celebrates small wins. Explains method calls by tracing the flow step by step.

intervention_triggers.idle_40_seconds: Hey! Methods can feel confusing at first because the code jumps around. Want me to trace the flow? I will show you: main calls methodA, which runs, returns a value, then main continues.
intervention_triggers.idle_90_seconds: No rush! Think of a method like a recipe card. It has a NAME (what it is called), INGREDIENTS (parameters), INSTRUCTIONS (body), and a RESULT (return value). Which part are you stuck on?
intervention_triggers.idle_180_seconds: Still here for you! Methods are a huge concept -- they change how you think about code. Let me break it into tiny pieces. First, just write the method signature (name + parameters). We will fill in the body together.
intervention_triggers.first_error: Not quite right, but methods are tricky at first! The most common mistake is mismatching return types. Let me trace through your code and show where it went off track.
intervention_triggers.repeated_errors: This one is challenging! Let me try a completely different explanation. Think of a method as a MACHINE: you feed it INPUT (parameters), it does WORK (body), and it gives you OUTPUT (return). What goes in? What comes out?
intervention_triggers.after_success: Brilliant! You wrote a clean, reusable method. This is how professional code is built -- small pieces that snap together. You are thinking like a real developer!

encouragement_phrases: Great method design! Clean, focused, reusable.
You nailed the parameters and return type!
Well done! That method does one thing and does it well.
Perfect! Your method signature is spot on.
Excellent! The DRY principle in action -- no repeated code!
Nice work! Parameters and arguments matched perfectly.
Awesome! You traced the method call correctly.
Great progress! Methods are a big milestone in programming.
Fantastic! Ready for more complex method patterns!

explanation_styles.analogy: A method is like a vending machine. You INSERT coins (arguments), PRESS a button (call the method), and GET a product (return value). The machine does its work internally -- you do not need to know how.
explanation_styles.visual: I draw the call flow: main() -> greet('Alice') -> enters greet method -> name='Alice' -> prints 'Hello Alice' -> returns to main. Like following arrows on a map.
explanation_styles.step_by_step: Step 1: Java sees the method CALL. Step 2: It JUMPS to the method definition. Step 3: Parameters get their values. Step 4: Body runs. Step 5: Return sends a value back. Step 6: Execution continues where it left off.
explanation_styles.code_trace: I highlight each line as it runs. main line 1: calls add(3,5). JUMP to add method. a=3, b=5. return 3+5=8. JUMP BACK to main. result=8.
explanation_styles.comparison: Without methods: 50 lines of repeated code. With methods: 10 lines + 5 reusable methods. Side by side shows the power of DRY.

theory.introduction:
Methods are where programming goes from 'writing code' to 'building software.' A method is a named, reusable block of code that performs a specific task. You write it once and call it as many times as you need, with different inputs each time.

Real-world analogy: Imagine you run a bakery. Instead of explaining the pancake recipe from scratch every morning, you write it on a card: 'Pancake Recipe: mix flour, eggs, milk. Pour on griddle. Flip at 2 minutes. Serves 4.' Now anyone can follow the card. They can double the ingredients (different parameters) or make it every day (call it many times). The recipe is the METHOD.

Without methods, every program would be one giant main() with hundreds of lines of tangled code. With methods, you break problems into small, named, testable pieces that snap together like building blocks.

In this lesson, you will learn to define your own methods, pass data in through parameters, get results back with return values, organise code with scope rules, create flexible interfaces with overloading, and even have methods call themselves with recursion. By the end, you will write code like a professional -- clean, organised, and reusable.
theory.why_important:
Methods are the most important organisational tool in programming. Here is why mastering them transforms your coding:

1. REUSABILITY: Write code once, call it everywhere. A calculateTax method works for every customer, every order, every time.

2. READABILITY: Well-named methods make code self-documenting. main() reads like a story: readInput(), validateData(), processOrder(), printReceipt().

3. DEBUGGING: When something breaks, you know which method is responsible. Fix it there, and every call site is fixed automatically.

4. TESTING: Small methods are easy to test. Does add(3,5) return 8? Does isValid(-1) return false? Quick to check.

5. TEAMWORK: Different developers can write different methods. They agree on signatures (name + parameters + return type) and work independently.

6. ABSTRACTION: Callers do not need to know HOW a method works -- just WHAT it does. Like using a vending machine without knowing its internal mechanics.

key_concepts.concept: What is a Method? -- Reusable Code Blocks
key_concepts.explanation:
A method is a named block of code that performs a specific task. You write it ONCE and call it as many times as you need. Methods are the building blocks of organised, professional code.

Real-world analogy: Think of a recipe card. A recipe for pancakes has a NAME ('Pancakes'), INGREDIENTS (flour, eggs, milk), STEPS (mix, pour, flip), and a RESULT (pancakes). You do not rewrite the recipe every time you want pancakes -- you just follow the card. Methods work the same way.

Why methods matter:
  1. DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself): Write code once, call many times
  2. Organisation: Break a big problem into small, manageable pieces
  3. Readability: Well-named methods make code self-documenting
  4. Testing: Small methods are easy to test and debug
  5. Reuse: Use the same method in different parts of your program

Every Java program starts with a method: public static void main(String[] args). Now you will learn to write your own.

Practice tip: If you find yourself copying and pasting code, that is a sign you need a method.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Method = reusable recipe. Define once, call many times. If you are copy-pasting code, make a method!

key_concepts.explanation:
Every method has four key parts:

1. ACCESS + MODIFIERS: static (for now) -- who can use this method
2. RETURN TYPE: what the method gives back (void = nothing, int = a number, String = text, etc.)
3. NAME: what you call it (use camelCase, verbs like calculateTotal, printReport, isValid)
4. PARAMETERS: what the method needs to work (in parentheses)

Format: static returnType methodName(paramType paramName) { body }

Examples:
  static void greet() -- returns nothing, needs nothing
  static int add(int a, int b) -- returns int, needs two ints
  static boolean isEven(int n) -- returns true/false, needs one int
  static String formatName(String first, String last) -- returns String, needs two Strings

The method SIGNATURE is the combination of name + parameter types. Java uses this to identify which method to call.

Naming convention: Methods should be verbs or verb phrases that describe what they DO. Good: calculateArea, printReport, isValid. Bad: data, thing, process1.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Four parts: modifier, return type, name, parameters. Name methods with verbs: calculateX, printY, isZ.

key_concepts.concept: void Methods -- Do Something, Return Nothing
key_concepts.explanation:
A void method performs an ACTION but does not give back a value. It is like asking someone to 'turn off the lights' -- they do the task, but they do not hand you anything back.

Use void when: printing output, modifying a display, logging information, or performing any side effect where no result is needed.

Key rules:
  - void methods CANNOT be used in expressions: int x = printHello(); is ILLEGAL
  - void methods CAN have an early return; (no value) to exit early
  - void methods are called as standalone statements: printHello();

Common beginner mistake: Trying to save the result of a void method. System.out.println() is void -- you cannot do String x = System.out.println('hi');

Practice tip: If your method just needs to DO something (print, log, display), use void. If it needs to GIVE something back (a number, a string, true/false), use a return type.
key_concepts.ai_hint: void = does something but returns nothing. Use for printing, logging, displaying. Cannot assign result to a variable.

key_concepts.explanation:
A non-void method COMPUTES something and gives it back to the caller using the return keyword. The caller can store the returned value, use it in an expression, or pass it to another method.

Real-world analogy: Asking a calculator 'What is 3 + 5?' The calculator RETURNS 8. You can then use that 8 however you want -- write it down, add more to it, or tell someone else.

Key rules:
  - The return type in the signature MUST match what return actually gives back
  - return immediately exits the method -- code after return is unreachable
  - Every path through the method MUST return a value (except void)
  - You can return expressions: return a + b; return x > 0;

Common mistake: Forgetting to capture the return value. add(3, 5); works but WASTES the result. Use: int sum = add(3, 5);

Another mistake: method says it returns int but some paths do not return. Java will give a compile error: 'missing return statement.'
key_concepts.ai_hint: return gives a value back to the caller. The type MUST match the signature. Every code path must return.

key_concepts.concept: Parameters and Arguments -- Feeding the Method
key_concepts.explanation:
PARAMETERS are the variables declared in the method definition (the recipe ingredients). ARGUMENTS are the actual values you pass when calling the method (the actual flour and eggs you use).

Format: static int add(int a, int b) -- a and b are PARAMETERS
        add(3, 5) -- 3 and 5 are ARGUMENTS

Java is PASS-BY-VALUE: when you pass an argument, Java copies the value into the parameter. Changing the parameter inside the method does NOT change the original variable outside.

Multiple parameters are separated by commas. The ORDER matters: the first argument goes to the first parameter, second to second, etc.

Type matching: If a method expects int, you must pass int (or something that auto-promotes to int). Passing a String where int is expected causes a compile error.

Practice tip: Think of parameters as INPUT slots. Each slot has a TYPE and a NAME. When you call the method, you fill each slot with a matching value.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Parameters = slots in definition. Arguments = values at call. Java copies values (pass-by-value). Order matters!

key_concepts.concept: Method Overloading -- Same Name, Different Parameters
key_concepts.explanation:
Method overloading means having MULTIPLE methods with the SAME name but DIFFERENT parameter lists. Java picks the right one based on what arguments you pass.

Real-world analogy: A print shop has a 'print' service. Print(document) prints a document. Print(photo, size) prints a photo at a specific size. Print(poster, size, quantity) prints posters in bulk. Same name, different inputs.

Rules:
  - Methods MUST differ in: number of parameters, OR types of parameters, OR order of types
  - Return type alone is NOT enough to overload (you cannot have two methods with same params but different return types)
  - Java resolves overloading at COMPILE time (not runtime)

Examples that WORK: add(int, int) and add(double, double) and add(int, int, int)
Examples that FAIL: add(int a, int b) and add(int x, int y) -- same parameter TYPES, different names is NOT overloading

The most common overloaded method you already use: System.out.println() accepts String, int, double, boolean, char, and more!
key_concepts.ai_hint: Same name, different parameters = overloading. Java picks by argument types. println() is overloaded too!

key_concepts.concept: Variable Scope -- Where Variables Live and Die
key_concepts.explanation:
Scope is the region of code where a variable exists and can be accessed. In Java:

1. LOCAL VARIABLES: Declared inside a method. Live only during that method call. Die when the method returns. Cannot be accessed from other methods.

2. PARAMETERS: Like local variables -- they live only during the method call.

3. BLOCK SCOPE: Variables declared inside { } (like inside a for loop or if block) exist only within those braces.

Real-world analogy: A hotel room key. Your key (local variable) works only in YOUR room (method). It does not work in other rooms. When you check out (method returns), the key is deactivated.

Key rules:
  - Two methods CAN have variables with the same name -- they are completely independent
  - Variables declared in a for loop header exist only inside that loop
  - You CANNOT access a variable before it is declared
  - Method parameters are NOT the same as the arguments passed in -- they are copies

Common mistake: Trying to use a variable from one method inside another method. Each method has its own 'room.'
key_concepts.ai_hint: Variables live inside their method/block only. Like hotel keys -- they only work in their own room.

key_concepts.explanation:
Java is ALWAYS pass-by-value. When you pass an argument to a method, Java makes a COPY of the value. The method works on the copy. The original is UNCHANGED.

Real-world analogy: You photocopy a document and give the copy to a colleague. They can write on the copy all they want -- your original document is untouched.

This means: if you pass int x = 5 to a method that does param = 10, x is STILL 5 after the call.

For objects (arrays, Strings), Java passes a copy of the REFERENCE (the address). The method can modify the object THROUGH the reference (like changing items in an array), but it cannot make the original variable point to a different object.

Key insight: Primitive types (int, double, boolean) are fully independent copies. Object references are copies of the address -- the object itself IS shared.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Java copies values. Primitives: fully independent. Arrays/objects: reference is copied, so the OBJECT can be changed.

key_concepts.concept: The DRY Principle -- Don't Repeat Yourself
key_concepts.ai_hint: Repeating code? Extract into a method. Fix once, works everywhere. DRY = professional programming.

key_concepts.explanation:
Recursion is when a method calls ITSELF. It sounds circular, but it works because each call handles a SMALLER version of the problem until it reaches a BASE CASE (the simplest version that can be answered directly).

Real-world analogy: Russian nesting dolls (Matryoshka). Open the biggest doll -- there is a smaller one inside. Open that -- even smaller. Keep going until you reach the tiniest doll that does not open (base case). Then you start putting them back together (returning).

Two requirements:
  1. BASE CASE: A condition where the method returns WITHOUT calling itself. Without this, infinite recursion!
  2. RECURSIVE CASE: The method calls itself with a SIMPLER input that moves toward the base case.

Classic example: factorial(5) = 5 * factorial(4) = 5 * 4 * factorial(3) = ... = 5 * 4 * 3 * 2 * 1 = 120

When to use recursion: tree structures, divide-and-conquer, mathematical definitions (factorial, Fibonacci), backtracking problems.

When NOT to use: simple loops. Recursion uses more memory (call stack) and is slower for simple counting.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Recursion = method calls itself with simpler input. MUST have base case or it runs forever (StackOverflow!).

key_concepts.explanation:
Each method should do ONE thing well. This is the Single Responsibility Principle (SRP).

BAD: A method that reads input, validates it, processes it, and prints the result. That is FOUR responsibilities.

GOOD: readInput() -> validateInput() -> processData() -> printResult(). Four focused methods that work together.

Guidelines:
  - If your method name needs 'and' (readAndProcess), split it
  - If the method body is longer than 15-20 lines, consider splitting
  - A method should be describable in ONE sentence
  - Input through PARAMETERS, output through RETURN values (avoid side effects)

Benefits: Each method is easy to understand, easy to test, easy to reuse, and easy to modify. Bugs are easier to find because each method is small.

Practice tip: Before writing code, plan your methods. What does each one DO? What does it NEED (parameters)? What does it GIVE BACK (return)?
key_concepts.ai_hint: One method, one job. If it needs 'and' in the name, split it. Small methods = clean code.

key_concepts.concept: The Call Stack -- How Methods Execute
key_concepts.explanation:
When a method is called, Java pushes a new FRAME onto the call stack. The frame holds the method's local variables and parameters. When the method returns, its frame is POPPED off the stack.

Real-world analogy: A stack of cafeteria trays. Each method call puts a new tray on top. When a method finishes, its tray is removed. You always work with the TOP tray (current method).

Call flow for main() -> a() -> b():
  Stack: [main] -> [main, a] -> [main, a, b] -> [main, a] -> [main] -> empty

StackOverflowError: If recursion has no base case (or recurses too deeply), the stack runs out of space. Java throws StackOverflowError. This is like stacking trays until the ceiling -- eventually you run out of room.

Debugging tip: When you see an error, the stack trace shows you the chain of method calls that led to the error. Read it bottom-to-top: the bottom is where it started, the top is where it crashed.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Call stack: each method call adds a frame. Return pops it. Too deep = StackOverflowError.

complete_examples.title: Example 1: Simple void Method
complete_examples.output:
Hello, Alice! Welcome.
Hello, Bob! Welcome.
Hello, Charlie! Welcome.
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
greet is defined once but called three times with different arguments. Each call: name gets the value passed, prints the personalised greeting, then returns to main. void means no return value.

Key takeaway: Define once, call many times with different arguments. That is the power of methods. This pattern appears constantly in real applications -- any time you need the same action with different data.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a second parameter 'role' (String) so it prints 'Hello, Alice! Welcome, you are a Student.' Can you overload greet with 1-param and 2-param versions?

complete_examples.title: Example 2: Method with Return Value
complete_examples.description: Method that computes and returns a result.
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
add returns an int. The caller stores it in s. multiply also returns int. The returned value can be used directly in another call: add(s, p) passes 30 and 30 to add.

Key takeaway: Return values let methods PRODUCE data that the caller can use however it wants -- store, print, pass to another method.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Write a subtract and divide method. Handle division by zero in divide (return 0 or print error).

complete_examples.title: Example 3: boolean Return -- Validation
complete_examples.description: Method that checks and returns true/false.
complete_examples.output:
4 even? true
7 even? false
25 in 1-100? true
Valid age 25? true
Valid age -5? false
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Boolean methods return true/false. Notice isValidAge CALLS isInRange -- methods calling methods is normal and powerful! The 'is' prefix naming convention tells you it returns boolean.

Key takeaway: Name boolean methods with is/has/can prefixes. They are perfect for conditions and validation.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Write isPositive, isNegative, and isZero. Then write classifyNumber that uses all three.

complete_examples.title: Example 4: Method Overloading
complete_examples.description: Same method name, different parameter lists.
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Three methods all named 'area' but with 1, 2, and 3 parameters. Java picks the right one based on arguments. area(5.0) calls circle. area(4.0, 6.0) calls rectangle. area(3.0, 4.0, 5.0) calls triangle (Heron's formula).

Key takeaway: Overloading lets you use one intuitive name for related operations. The compiler picks the right version automatically.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add area(double side) for a square. How does Java distinguish it from circle area(double radius)? It cannot -- same signature! You would need a different name like squareArea.

complete_examples.title: Example 5: Scope Demonstration
complete_examples.description: Variables live only in their method.
complete_examples.output:
method1: x=100 y=10
method2: x=100 y=20
main: x=100
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
x is class-level (static field) -- accessible everywhere. Each method has its own y -- they are completely separate variables. main cannot access y from method1 or method2.

Key takeaway: Local variables are private to their method. Class-level variables are shared. Keep variables as LOCAL as possible for clean code.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Try declaring int z inside main and accessing it from method1. What happens? Compile error -- scope violation.

complete_examples.title: Example 6: Pass-by-Value Proof
complete_examples.output:
Inside: num=999 text=CHANGED
After: num=42 text=original
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Inside the method, num and text are COPIES. Changing them to 999 and 'CHANGED' only affects the copies. The originals myNum and myText are untouched. String is immutable so even though it is an object, reassigning the parameter does not change the original.

Key takeaway: Java always passes copies. Changing a parameter inside a method never changes the original variable outside.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Now try passing an int[] array and changing arr[0] inside the method. The change STICKS because the array object is shared via the reference copy.

complete_examples.title: Example 7: Methods Calling Methods
complete_examples.description: Building complex behaviour from simple methods.
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
printWeather calls two helper methods: celsiusToFahrenheit and tempCategory. Each method does ONE thing. Together they create a rich output. This is method COMPOSITION.

Key takeaway: Build complex behaviour by composing simple methods. Each method is small, focused, and testable.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a windChill method and incorporate it into printWeather when temperature is below 10C.

complete_examples.title: Example 8: Array as Parameter and Return
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
findMax takes an array, returns a single int (the maximum). doubleAll takes an array, returns a NEW array with doubled values. The original nums array is unchanged.

Key takeaway: Methods can accept and return arrays. Create NEW arrays for results to avoid modifying the original.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Write findMin and a method that returns only the even elements as a new array.

complete_examples.title: Example 9: Recursion -- Factorial
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
factorial(5): 5*factorial(4) -> 5*4*factorial(3) -> 5*4*3*factorial(2) -> 5*4*3*2*factorial(1) -> 5*4*3*2*1 = 120. Each call simplifies the problem. Base case (n<=1) stops the recursion.

Key takeaway: Recursion needs: base case (when to stop) + recursive case (call with simpler input).
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Write recursive sum(n) that returns 1+2+...+n. What is the base case? What is the recursive step?

complete_examples.title: Example 10: Recursion -- Fibonacci
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
fib(5) = fib(4)+fib(3) = (fib(3)+fib(2))+(fib(2)+fib(1)) = ... Each call branches into two more calls. This is simple but SLOW for large n (exponential time). A loop version is much faster.

Key takeaway: Recursion is elegant but can be slow. Fibonacci is a classic case where loop version is much more efficient.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Time fib(30) vs fib(40). Notice the massive slowdown? Write an iterative (loop) version and compare speed.

complete_examples.title: Example 11: Helper Methods -- Clean main()
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
main is clean and readable: print header, calculate average, get grade, display. Each method is small and focused. If you need to change grading rules, change ONE method. If you need a different header style, change ONE method.

Key takeaway: A clean main() method reads like a story. Helper methods do the actual work. This is professional code structure.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a printScores method that prints each score with its rank. Then add findHighest and findLowest methods.

complete_examples.title: Example 12: Varargs -- Variable Arguments
complete_examples.description: Methods accepting any number of arguments.
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
int... numbers means 'zero or more ints'. Inside the method, numbers is an array. You can pass any count of arguments. Varargs must be the LAST parameter.

Key takeaway: Varargs (Type... name) lets a method accept any number of arguments. Treated as an array inside.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Write a method max(int... numbers) that returns the largest. What about min and average?

complete_examples.title: Example 13: Method Chaining Concept
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
The return value of one method feeds directly into another: addBang(upper(trim(input))). This reads inside-out: trim first, then upper, then addBang. Both approaches give the same result.

Key takeaway: When methods return values, you can chain them. Each method transforms the data and passes it along.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a method replace(String s, String old, String newStr). Chain it to replace 'WORLD' with 'JAVA'.

complete_examples.title: Example 14: Complete Mini-Program
complete_examples.description: All concepts combined in one program.
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Four focused methods: isPrime (checks one number), countPrimes (counts primes up to limit), getPrimes (collects primes into array), printArray (formats output). main is clean and readable. Each method does ONE thing.

Key takeaway: Professional programs are built from many small, focused methods that call each other.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add sumPrimes and averagePrime methods. Then add a method to find twin primes (primes that differ by 2, like 11 and 13).

visual_diagram.title: Java Methods -- Code Organisation Visual Guide
visual_diagram.content: JAVA METHODS -- COMPLETE VISUAL GUIDE How Java organises code into reusable blocks ======== [1] METHOD ANATOMY ======== static int add(int a, int b) { modifier return name parameters return a + b; <-- body (the work) } ======== [2] METHOD CALL FLOW ======== main() | calls add(3, 5) v add(a=3, b=5) | computes 3+5=8 | return 8 v main() receives 8 ======== [3] void vs RETURN ======== void Does action, returns NOTHING greet("Alice") -> prints, done int/String/etc Does work, returns VALUE add(3,5) -> returns 8 to caller ======== [4] OVERLOADING ======== add(int, int) -> Java picks by argument types add(double, double) -> different param types = OK add(int, int, int) -> different param count = OK add(int x, int y) -> same types, diff names = NOT overloading! ======== [5] SCOPE ======== Class level static int x = 10; accessible everywhere Method level int y = 5; only inside this method Block level for(int i...) { } only inside these braces ======== [6] PASS-BY-VALUE ======== Primitives (int, double, boolean): Value is COPIED. Changes inside method do NOT affect original. Objects (arrays, Strings): Reference is COPIED. Object itself IS shared. Array contents CAN be changed. String is immutable. ======== [7] RECURSION ======== factorial(5) = 5 * factorial(4) = 5 * 4 * factorial(3) = 5 * 4 * 3 * factorial(2) = 5 * 4 * 3 * 2 * factorial(1) = 5 * 4 * 3 * 2 * 1 (BASE CASE) = 120 ======== [8] METHOD DESIGN RULES ======== * One method = one job (Single Responsibility) * Name with verbs: calculate, print, is, has, get, set * Keep methods short (under 20 lines ideal) * Input through parameters, output through return * Avoid side effects -- methods should be predictable * Test each method independently ======== [9] COMMON BUGS ======== BUG: Missing return statement FIX: Every code path must return (or method is void) BUG: Return type mismatch FIX: return 3.14 in an int method -> error. Use double. BUG: Forgetting to capture return FIX: add(3,5); wastes result. Use: int x = add(3,5); BUG: Using = instead of == in boolean return FIX: return x = 5; assigns. return x == 5; compares. BUG: Infinite recursion FIX: No base case -> StackOverflowError. Always check base first. BUG: Variable not in scope FIX: Variable from one method cannot be used in another. BUG: Wrong argument order FIX: add(name, age) vs add(age, name) -- order must match params. [REMEMBER] * Methods = reusable code blocks. Define once, call many times. * void = no return. int/String/etc = returns a value. * Parameters are COPIES (pass-by-value). * Overloading = same name, different parameter types/count. * Scope: variables die when their block/method ends. * Recursion needs a BASE CASE or it runs forever. * DRY: if you copy-paste code, make a method instead. ======== WHEN TO CREATE A METHOD ======== YES, make a method when: * Code appears more than once * Logic is complex and deserves a name * You want to test a piece of logic independently * main() is getting longer than 20 lines * You can describe the task in one sentence NO, skip when: * Code runs once and is trivially simple * Creating a method adds more complexity than it removes * The method would have 10+ parameters (design smell)

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 1: Simple Greeting
practice_exercises.description: Write a void method that prints a greeting. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Your very first method! Just print a message. You have got this!

hints.message: static void greet() { System.out.println(...); }
void means no return. Just print inside the method body.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 2: Greeting with Name
practice_exercises.description: Write a void method with a String parameter. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Add a parameter to personalise the greeting!

hints.message: static void greet(String name) { println("Hello, " + name + "!"); }
name is the parameter. It receives the value from the call: greet("Alice") -> name="Alice".

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 3: Print Line Separator
practice_exercises.description: Write a method that prints a line of dashes. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: A reusable separator -- call it whenever you need a visual break!

hints.message: Use a for loop inside: for(int i=0;i<length;i++) print('-');
After the loop, println() for a newline.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 4: Print Box
practice_exercises.description: Draw a box of a given size. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Combine a loop with your method to draw a box!

hints.message: Row 1 and last: all stars. Middle rows: star + spaces + star.
for each row: if first/last row, print all stars. Else star + (width-2) spaces + star.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 5: Print Array
practice_exercises.description: Write a method to print array contents. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: No more writing the print loop every time -- make a method!

hints.message: Print '[', then loop with comma separator, then ']'.
if(i>0) print(", ") before each element after the first.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 6: Repeat Message
practice_exercises.description: Print a message n times. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Repetition + methods = super useful combo!

hints.message: for(int i=0; i<times; i++) println(msg);
Simple loop inside the method. Two parameters: what to print and how many times.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 7: Print Multiplication Table
practice_exercises.description: Print times table for a number. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Wrap the multiplication table in a reusable method!

hints.message: for(int i=1;i<=12;i++) println(n+" x "+i+" = "+(n*i));
Straightforward loop. n is the parameter, i is the multiplier.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 8: Print Stars Triangle
practice_exercises.description: Print a right triangle of stars. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Reuse your loop skills inside a method!

hints.message: for(int i=1;i<=rows;i++){for(int j=0;j<i;j++)print('*');println();}
Nested loop: outer for rows, inner for stars per row.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 9: Swap Array Elements
practice_exercises.description: Write a void method to swap two array elements. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Arrays are passed by reference -- changes inside the method stick!

hints.message: int temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[j]; arr[j] = temp;
Arrays are objects -- the method modifies the ACTUAL array, not a copy.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 10: void with Early Return
practice_exercises.description: Use return in a void method to exit early. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: void methods can use return; (no value) to exit early -- like a guard clause!

hints.message: if(n <= 0) return; then println(n + " is positive");
return; (no value) exits the void method immediately. Guard clause pattern.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 11: Add Two Numbers
practice_exercises.description: Return the sum of two integers. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: The classic first return method!

hints.message: static int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }
return sends the computed value back to the caller.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 12: Maximum of Two
practice_exercises.description: Return the larger of two numbers. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Compare and return the winner!

hints.message: return (a > b) ? a : b; or use if/else.
Ternary is clean for simple 2-way returns.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 13: Is Even Check
practice_exercises.description: Return boolean for even check. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Boolean returns are perfect for validation!

hints.message: return n % 2 == 0; -- the comparison IS the boolean.
Do not write if(n%2==0) return true; else return false; -- that is redundant.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 14: Circle Area
practice_exercises.description: Calculate and return circle area. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Use Math.PI in your return calculation!

hints.message: return Math.PI * radius * radius;
PI * r^2. Math.PI gives 3.14159...

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 15: Celsius to Fahrenheit
practice_exercises.description: Convert temperature and return result. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Formula: F = C * 9/5 + 32. Return the result!

hints.message: return celsius * 9.0 / 5.0 + 32;
Use 9.0/5.0 for double division, not 9/5 (integer division!).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 16: Count Vowels
practice_exercises.description: Return the number of vowels in a string. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Loop through chars, count matches, return the count!

hints.message: int count=0; for each char: if 'aeiouAEIOU' contains it, count++. Return count.
Use String.indexOf(ch) >= 0 to check if char is a vowel.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 17: Power Calculator
practice_exercises.description: Return base raised to exponent. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Repeated multiplication in a loop, return the result!

hints.message: long result=1; for(int i=0;i<exp;i++) result*=base; return result;
Use long to handle big results. Start at 1 not 0!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 18: Reverse String
practice_exercises.description: Return a reversed copy of a string. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Build the result string backwards and return it!

hints.message: String rev=""; for(int i=s.length()-1;i>=0;i--) rev+=s.charAt(i); return rev;
Loop backwards, append each character to build reversed string.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 19: Array Sum
practice_exercises.description: Return the sum of all elements in an array. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Accumulator pattern inside a method!

hints.message: int sum=0; for(int n:arr) sum+=n; return sum;
Classic accumulator. for-each is cleanest here.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 20: Find in Array
practice_exercises.description: Return index of value, or -1 if not found. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Search pattern: loop, check, return index or -1!

hints.message: for(int i=0;i<arr.length;i++) if(arr[i]==target) return i; return -1;
return inside loop exits immediately when found. -1 at end means not found.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 21: Is Palindrome
practice_exercises.description: Return whether a string is a palindrome. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Compare first with last, second with second-last!

hints.message: for(int i=0;i<s.length()/2;i++) if(s.charAt(i)!=s.charAt(s.length()-1-i)) return false; return true;
Any mismatch -> return false immediately. If loop finishes, all matched -> true.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 22: Grade Assigner
practice_exercises.description: Return letter grade from score. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Multiple return statements for different ranges!

hints.message: if(score>=90) return "A"; if(score>=80) return "B"; ...
Each return exits the method. No need for else after return.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 23: Overloaded add
practice_exercises.description: Two versions: int and double. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Same name, different types -- Java picks the right one!

hints.message: Write two separate methods. Java matches by argument type.
add(3,5) -> int version. add(1.5,2.5) -> double version.

static int add(int a, int b) { return a+b; }
static double add(double a, double b) { return a+b; }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 24: Overloaded max
practice_exercises.description: max for 2 and 3 parameters. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Find the maximum of 2 OR 3 numbers with the same method name!

hints.message: max(a,b): return a>b?a:b. max(a,b,c): return max(max(a,b),c);
The 3-param version can CALL the 2-param version! Reuse!

static int max(int a, int b) { return a>b?a:b; }
static int max(int a, int b, int c) { return max(max(a,b),c); }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 25: Overloaded greet
practice_exercises.description: Different greetings based on parameters. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: One version with just name, another with name + title!

hints.message: greet(name): "Hello, "+name. greet(title, name): "Hello, "+title+" "+name.
Different parameter COUNT = valid overloading.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 26: Overloaded print
practice_exercises.description: Print int, double, and String differently. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Format output differently based on the type!

hints.message: display(int n): "Integer: "+n. display(double d): printf. display(String s): "Text: "+s.
Three methods, three types. Java picks by argument type.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 27: Overloaded repeat
practice_exercises.description: Repeat with default count. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: One version with count, one with default count of 3!

hints.message: repeat(msg) calls repeat(msg, 3); -- reuse the detailed version!
The no-count version delegates to the count version with default 3.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 28: Overloaded average
practice_exercises.description: Average of array OR of two/three values. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Multiple ways to calculate an average!

hints.message: Two-param: (a+b)/2.0. Three-param: (a+b+c)/3.0. Array: sum/length.
Use 2.0, 3.0 for double division!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 29: Overloaded contains
practice_exercises.description: Check containment in String and array. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Same concept (contains), different data types!

hints.message: String: s.indexOf(c)>=0. Array: loop and check.
Different parameter TYPES = valid overloading.

static boolean contains(String s, char c){return s.indexOf(c)>=0;}
static boolean contains(int[] a, int v){for(int n:a)if(n==v)return true;return false;}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 30: Overloaded format
practice_exercises.description: Format numbers as currency or percentage. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Same name, completely different formatting based on parameters!

hints.message: format(amount, currency): String.format("%s%.2f", currency, amount). format(ratio): ratio*100 + "%".
Different parameter count AND types.

static String format(double amt, String cur){return String.format("%s%.2f",cur,amt);}
static String format(double ratio){return String.format("%.1f%%",ratio*100);}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 31: Scope Test
practice_exercises.description: Predict output with local variables. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Each method has its own variables -- even if the names match!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Before: x = 5
changeX: x = 99
After: x = 5

hints.message: changeX creates its OWN x=99. main's x=5 is a completely different variable.
Same name does not mean same variable. Scope is per-method.
// No code change needed. The output shows scope: main's x stays 5.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 32: Pass-by-Value Test
practice_exercises.description: Show that primitives are not changed. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Java copies values -- the original is safe!

hints.message: n is a COPY of x. Changing n to 20 does not affect x.
Pass-by-value: the method gets a photocopy, not the original.
// x stays 10 because Java copies the value into n.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 33: Array Modification
practice_exercises.description: Show arrays CAN be changed by methods. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Arrays are different -- the reference is copied but the object is shared!

hints.message: The array OBJECT is shared. arr[i]*=2 changes the actual array elements.
Reference copied -> same object. Changes to contents are visible outside.
// Array IS modified because both variables point to the same object.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 34: Return Instead of Modify
practice_exercises.description: Return a new value instead of trying to change a parameter. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Since primitives are copies, RETURN the new value instead!

hints.message: static int doubleIt(int n) { return n * 2; }
Instead of modifying the copy (impossible), return the new value and reassign.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 35: Block Scope
practice_exercises.description: Variables declared in blocks die at the closing brace. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Curly braces define variable lifetime!

hints.message: i and temp exist only inside the for loop braces. Outside, they are gone.
Block scope: variable lives from declaration to closing }.
// Both i and temp are out of scope after the for loop's closing brace.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 36: Multiple Returns
practice_exercises.description: Method with multiple return points. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Each return exits immediately -- use for guard clauses!

hints.message: if(n<0) return "Negative"; if(n==0) return "Zero"; return "Positive";
Each return exits the method. No else needed after return.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 37: Scope Shadowing
practice_exercises.description: Local variable hides class variable. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: When local and class variables have the same name, local wins!

hints.message: Local 'value' hides class 'value'. Use ClassName.value to access class-level.
Shadowing is legal but confusing. Best practice: use different names.

// Inside test(), 'value' refers to local variable (5).
// Class-level 'value' (100) is accessed via Ex37.value

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 38: Variable Lifetime
practice_exercises.description: Track when variables are created and destroyed. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Variables are born at declaration and die at their scope's closing brace!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Start
i=0 inner=0
i=1 inner=10
End

hints.message: i and inner are created fresh each iteration and destroyed at the loop end.
inner is created 2 times (once per iteration). Each time it starts fresh.
// i: born at for, dies at }. inner: born each iteration, dies each iteration.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 39: Recursive Countdown
practice_exercises.description: Count down recursively. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Replace the loop with self-calling -- your first recursion!

hints.message: if(n<=0){println("Blast off!");return;} println(n); countdown(n-1);
Base case: n<=0 prints blast off and returns. Recursive: print n, call countdown(n-1).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 40: Recursive Sum
practice_exercises.description: Sum 1 to n recursively. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.

hints.message: Base: if(n<=0) return 0; Recursive: return n + sum(n-1);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 41: Recursive Factorial
practice_exercises.description: Calculate n! recursively. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: The classic recursion example: n! = n * (n-1)!

hints.message: Base: if(n<=1) return 1; Recursive: return n * factorial(n-1);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 42: Recursive Power
practice_exercises.description: Calculate base^exp recursively. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.

hints.message: Base: if(exp==0) return 1; Recursive: return base * power(base, exp-1);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 43: Recursive String Reverse
practice_exercises.description: Reverse a string recursively. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Take the last char, then reverse the rest!

hints.message: Base: if(s.length()<=1) return s; Recursive: return reverse(s.substring(1)) + s.charAt(0);
Take first char, put it at the END of the reversed rest.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 44: Recursive Fibonacci
practice_exercises.description: Return nth Fibonacci number. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: fib(n) = fib(n-1) + fib(n-2). Two base cases!

hints.message: Base: if(n<=0) return 0; if(n==1) return 1; Recursive: return fib(n-1)+fib(n-2);
Two base cases because fib needs two previous values.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 45: Recursive Palindrome
practice_exercises.description: Check palindrome recursively. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: If first==last, check the middle recursively!

hints.message: Base: length<=1 return true. If first!=last return false. Else check substring(1,len-1).
Peel off first and last chars. If they match, recurse on the middle.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 46: Recursive GCD
practice_exercises.description: Euclidean algorithm recursively. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: gcd(a,b) = gcd(b, a%b). Base: b==0 -> return a.

hints.message: if(b==0) return a; return gcd(b, a%b);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 47: Recursive Count Digits
practice_exercises.description: Count digits in a number recursively. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Remove one digit each call: n/10. Count how many removals until 0!

hints.message: Base: if(n<10) return 1; Recursive: return 1 + countDigits(n/10);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 48: Recursive Binary Conversion
practice_exercises.description: Convert decimal to binary string recursively. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: n%2 gives last bit. Recurse on n/2 for remaining bits!

hints.message: Base: if(n<=1) return ""+n; Recursive: return toBinary(n/2) + (n%2);
42/2=21 rem 0. 21/2=10 rem 1. ... Build string left to right.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 49: Validation Methods
practice_exercises.description: Build input validators. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Methods that return boolean are perfect for validation!

hints.message: isValidEmail: contains @ and . isValidAge: 0-150. isStrongPassword: length>=6.
Each method checks ONE thing and returns boolean.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 50: String Utilities
practice_exercises.description: Build a mini string library. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Methods as a reusable toolkit!

hints.message: capitalize: charAt(0) toUpper + substring(1). countWords: split(" ").length. truncate: substring(0,max)+"...".
Each method is small and focused -- a utility library.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 51: Math Utilities
practice_exercises.description: Build math helper methods. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Your own Math library!

hints.message: clamp: if<min return min, if>max return max, else val. map: proportional scaling.
clamp keeps value in bounds. map converts between ranges.

static int clamp(int v,int min,int max){return v<min?min:v>max?max:v;}
static double map(double v,double mn1,double mx1,double mn2,double mx2){return mn2+(v-mn1)*(mx2-mn2)/(mx1-mn1);}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 52: Array Toolkit
practice_exercises.description: Build array helper methods. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Methods that process arrays -- the most common real-world pattern!

hints.message: min/max: tracker pattern. average: sum/length. contains: search pattern.
Four focused methods, each one simple. Together they are powerful.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 53: Password Generator
practice_exercises.description: Generate a random password of given length. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Combine chars from different pools into a password!

hints.message: String chars="ABCabc123!@#"; loop length times, pick random char.
Use (int)(Math.random()*chars.length()) for random index.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 54: Receipt Printer
practice_exercises.description: Build a receipt with multiple helper methods. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Compose multiple methods to build a complete receipt!

hints.message: Loop items, use padRight and formatPrice. Sum prices for total.
Compose helper methods for clean output.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 55: Number Analyser
practice_exercises.description: Multiple analysis methods on one number. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Build a toolkit of number-checking methods!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
97 prime: true
97 even: false
97 digit sum: 16
97 digits: 2

hints.message: isPrime: loop 2 to sqrt(n). isEven: n%2==0. digitSum: loop %10 and /10. digitCount: loop /10.
Each method handles one analysis. Together they give a full profile.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 56: Caesar Cipher Methods
practice_exercises.description: Encrypt and decrypt with methods. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Separate encrypt and decrypt into clean methods!

hints.message: encrypt: shift each char forward. decrypt: shift backward (or encrypt with 26-shift).
decrypt can call encrypt(msg, 26-shift)! Method reuse!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 57: Sort Helper
practice_exercises.description: Build a sorting method with helpers. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Compose swap + findMin + sort methods!

hints.message: findMinIndex: loop from start, track minimum index. selectionSort: for each position, findMinIndex and swap.
Selection sort: find smallest, put in position 0. Find next smallest, position 1. Etc.

static int findMinIndex(int[]a,int s){int mi=s;for(int i=s+1;i<a.length;i++)if(a[i]<a[mi])mi=i;return mi;}
static void selectionSort(int[]a){for(int i=0;i<a.length-1;i++)swap(a,i,findMinIndex(a,i));}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 58: Mini Calculator
practice_exercises.description: Complete calculator with method per operation. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Each operation is a method. A dispatcher calls the right one!

hints.message: switch(op){case '+':return add(a,b); case '-':return sub(a,b); ...}
The calc method DISPATCHES to the right operation method.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 59: Student Grade Report
practice_exercises.description: Complete program with multiple methods. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: The big challenge: compose 5+ methods into a working program!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
=== Alice's Report ===
Scores: 85 92 78 90 88
Average: 86.6
Grade: B
Highest: 92
Lowest: 78

hints.message: calcAverage: sum/length. getGrade: if-else chain. getHighest/Lowest: tracker pattern.
printReport calls all helper methods and formats the output.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 60: Recursive vs Iterative
practice_exercises.description: Compare recursive and loop solutions. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: The ultimate challenge: write BOTH versions and compare!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Fact loop: 3628800
Fact recur: 3628800
Fib loop: 55
Fib recur: 55

hints.message: factLoop: long r=1; for(i=1..n) r*=i. factRecur: if(n<=1) return 1; return n*factRecur(n-1).
fibLoop: two variables tracking previous values. fibRecur: fib(n-1)+fib(n-2).
